reasoning: German Chancellors very rarely leave office within their first year due to the stability provided by the Basic Law, notably the Constructive Vote of No Confidence which makes it extremely difficult to oust a sitting Chancellor without a verified successor. Furthermore, Friedrich Merz has pursued the Chancellorship for roughly two decades; he is extremely unlikely to resign voluntarily shortly after achieving it. While he would be close to 70 in 2025, the baseline probability of sudden death, debilitating health emergencies, or massive scandals forcing immediate resignation within an approximately 8-month window (May 6 to December 31) for a major head of government in a developed nation is around 1-3%. The political dynamic in Germany strongly points to Merz securing a relatively stable coalition (likely with the SPD or in a Jamaica format) following the expected early 2025 federal elections, insulating him from quick parliamentary removal. Even if his coalition breaks down early, he would constitutionally remain as acting Chancellor until the Bundestag formally elects someone else. Thus, the probability of him ceasing to be Chancellor in this timeframe is exceptionally low.
